text: The laparoscopic view shows the uterus with an anterior myoma measuring three centimeters, classified as type six, and a posterior cyst measuring nine centimeters. The pouch of Douglas is visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon opens the retroperitoneal space by retracting the sigmoid colon medially and cutting its peritoneal attachments. The retroperitoneal space is expanded, and connective tissues are removed to locate the uterine artery at its origin.

The surgeon identifies the umbilical artery, removing surrounding fatty tissue to expose it. The umbilical artery is tracked, revealing tension in its upper position. The paravesical fossa is visible, with the ureter positioned medially to the umbilical artery. The ureter is observed rotating, and the uterine artery is located between the ureter and the umbilical artery. The paramed rectal fossa is also visible, with clear identification of the ureter, pararectal fossa, and uterine artery.

The surgeon clips the uterine artery, a pulsatile vessel, in the medial part of the paramedical fossa. The anatomy is displayed with the uterus, umbilical artery, uterine vein, paravesical fossa, and pararectal fossa visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon performs the procedure on the left side of the retroperitoneal space, retracting the peritoneum medially to open the space. Care is taken to avoid damaging a large vein with visible varicosities in the infundibulopelvic region. The surgeon uses forceps to gently retract the vein and progressively increase the space.

The surgeon increases the retroperitoneal space and coagulates the connective tissue. The external iliac vessel is visible, and a hole is created between the external iliac vessel and the posterior aspect of the round ligament using traction and contraction movements. The goal is to locate the umbilical artery while minimizing bleeding during dissection.

The surgeon increases the space in the retroperitoneal area and identifies the umbilical artery, applying traction to visualize the vessel clearly. The ureter is located and its rotation is observed. The pararectal and paravesical fossae are visible, along with the uterine artery. A clip is placed at the origin of the uterine artery, and the rotation of the uterus is noted. The laparoscopic myomectomy procedure is set to begin.

The surgeon identifies a large posterior myoma and performs a longitudinal incision using classical bipolar devices. The incision is initially opened transversally before proceeding longitudinally, cutting through the tissue in a bloodless manner.

The surgeon uses a 10mm laparoscopic forceps to apply strong traction on the myoma, aiming to remove it from its bed. The forceps are repositioned deeper to enhance traction and contraction, following a classical myomectomy technique.

The surgeon progressively extracts the myoma from its bed, using hydro-dissection to separate the myoma from surrounding tissues. Fluid is introduced between the myoma and healthy tissue to identify a clear dissection plane, with forceps repositioned to optimize traction.

The surgeon uses forceps to apply traction on the myoma, repositioning them frequently to optimize traction. Hydro-dissection is employed to separate the myoma from surrounding tissues, using fluid to identify the dissection plane. The surgeon removes connective tissues while alternating between water and energy techniques.
The surgeon is nearing the completion of extracting a large myoma from its bed, using laparoscopic techniques to carefully separate the myoma from surrounding tissues.

The surgeon completes hemostasis, revealing a large vein adjacent to the extracted myoma. The myoma is positioned for morcellation, with a 14-millimeter needle and a large wheel instrument visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon performs an intracorporeal knotting technique to secure a hemostatic knot, placing the healthy myometrium inside the myoma bed. The knot is tied approximately two centimeters from the scar, resulting in the embagination of tissue within the myoma bed to achieve effective hemostasis.

The surgeon invaginates the tissue into the myoma bed, while the assistant temporarily locks the knot. The surgeon then ties a second and third knot using an intracorporeal knotting technique, ensuring the knots are placed three centimeters away from the scar to maintain hemostasis.

The surgeon performs a double square knot technique, placing healthy myometrium inside the myoma bed. The stitches are tied tightly, indicated by visible ischemia near the edges of the myoma scar. A stitch is placed near the punch of the blast, with the needle inserted from lateral to internal positions, demonstrating a change in needle placement.
The surgeon places healthy myometrial tissue into the myoma bed using a double square knot technique, ensuring the tissue is invaginated as far as possible for optimal hemostasis.
The surgeon ties the last knot on the myoma, ensuring careful placement to avoid incorporating the tube into the stitch.

The surgeon uses the same technique to place healthy myometrial tissue inside the myoma bed, ensuring the invaginated tissue is well peritonealized. The edges of the uterus show good ischemia, indicating effective hemostasis. The surgical field is free of deperitonization areas, and fluid is removed from the pelvis to reduce the risk of postoperative adhesion.
The surgeon removes the anterior myoma using a combination of hydro-dissection and traction-counter traction techniques, visibly manipulating the tissue to facilitate extraction.
The surgeon places the second myoma into a glass punch and rapidly closes the lid. Two stitches are applied to secure the small myoma.

The surgeon uses a double square knot technique to secure the tissue, ensuring the knot is locked and traction is applied on the opposite side, maintaining a distance from the edge of the surgical site.
The surgeon uses a 14mm needle with Vicryl 1 suture to tie a strong knot, maintaining a distance from the scar tissue.
The surgeon places the excised tissue into an endo bag for removal. The anterior and posterior scars are inspected, showing well-tied knots and visible ischemia with no bleeding present.
The surgeon prepares for morcellation by placing a large 500 cc endoscopic bag into the pelvic cavity, following new recommendations for contained morcellation.

The surgeon places the small myoma into the endoscopic bag, followed by the second myoma, ensuring they are well-covered. The morcellator is set to 800 RPM and inserted into the bag, where the surgeon carefully follows and pushes the myoma against the bag during morcellation.

The surgeon completes the morcellation of a small myoma within an endoscopic bag, reducing the risk of parasitic myoma formation. Small pieces of the myoma are visible inside the bag after morcellation.

The surgeon removes all liquid from the surgical field, confirming the absence of small myoma pieces. The anterior and posterior scars are inspected, showing good hemostasis with no bleeding. The uterine artery is visualized with a clip in place, indicating complete revascularization.
